narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of first-time bar exam success based on students' academic and background profiles, estimated a 72.8% chance that you would not pass the bar on your first attempt. This high predicted risk of failure directly led to the rejection, as the university prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed in their legal careers. For context, the model looks at patterns from thousands of past students, including their grades, test scores, and family backgrounds, to make these forecasts. In your case, as a Black male from a lower-middle income family, your profile stood out in ways that heavily influenced the decision, with your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1—far below the typical 5.5—being the strongest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, as it signals consistently lower performance relative to peers. Your LSAT score of 25.5, well under the average of 36.7, was the second-biggest driver in the same direction, highlighting a key area where the model sees elevated risk compared to most applicants. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, close to the average of 3.21, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing success. Your plan to attend full-time, like the vast majority of students at 92.7%, didn't sway things much either way. Overall, these top factors—especially your later-year grades and LSAT—outweighed the strengths in your profile, placing you outside the typical successful applicant who tends to rank higher in school deciles around 5-6 and scores much stronger on the LSAT. While your undergraduate performance shows promise, the model weighed recent academic trends more heavily here. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven call to protect the program's outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly above average, strengthening your Year 3 grades through additional coursework or tutoring, and reapplying once you've addressed these gaps—many students in similar situations have succeeded by targeting these exact areas.
